source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes. It should be started at diagnosis alongside lifestyle modification unless contraindicated, and continued as long as it is tolerated and not contraindicated.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Pharmacologic Approaches to Glycemic Treatment: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: In patients with type 2 diabetes and established or high risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor with proven benefit is recommended independent of HbA1c and independent of metformin use.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: A GLP-1 receptor agonist with proven cardiovascular benefit is recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or indicators of high cardiovascular risk, and is preferred where weight management is a priority.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: Metformin is contraindicated in patients with an eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73 m2. It should not be initiated when eGFR is 30-45 mL/min/1.73 m2, and the dose should be reduced and renal function monitored in that range.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: When additional glycemic lowering is needed and weight neutrality with low hypoglycemia risk is desired, a DPP-4 inhibitor may be added. DPP-4 inhibitors should not be combined with GLP-1 receptor agonists.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: Sulfonylureas are an effective and low-cost option to intensify glycemic control but carry a higher risk of hypoglycemia and weight gain; use with caution in older adults and those at risk of hypoglycemia.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: Insulin should be considered when there is evidence of ongoing catabolism, symptoms of hyperglycemia, or when HbA1c or blood glucose levels are very high (HbA1c above 10 percent), either initially or as add-on therapy.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

text: In the general nonblack population, initial antihypertensive treatment should include a thiazide-type diuretic, calcium channel blocker, ACE inhibitor, or angiotensin receptor blocker. Goal blood pressure is below 140/90 mmHg in adults younger than 60 years.
citation: James PA, et al. 2014 Evidence-Based Guideline for the Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults (JNC 8). JAMA. 2014;311(5):507-520.

text: In patients with diabetes or chronic kidney disease, initial or add-on antihypertensive therapy should include an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker to improve kidney outcomes, regardless of race.
citation: James PA, et al. 2014 Evidence-Based Guideline for the Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults (JNC 8). JAMA. 2014;311(5):507-520.

text: For adults with confirmed hypertension and known cardiovascular disease or a 10-year ASCVD risk of 10 percent or higher, a blood pressure target of less than 130/80 mmHg is recommended. First-line agents include thiazide diuretics, CCBs, and ACE inhibitors or ARBs.
citation: Whelton PK, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Hypertension. 2018;71(6):e13-e115.

text: In black adults with hypertension but without heart failure or chronic kidney disease, initial antihypertensive treatment should include a thiazide-type diuretic or calcium channel blocker.
citation: Whelton PK, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Hypertension. 2018;71(6):e13-e115.

text: In adults 40 to 75 years of age with diabetes mellitus, a moderate-intensity statin should be initiated regardless of estimated 10-year ASCVD risk. A high-intensity statin is reasonable for those with multiple ASCVD risk factors.
citation: Grundy SM, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.

text: In patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, high-intensity statin therapy should be initiated or continued with the aim of achieving a 50 percent or greater reduction in LDL cholesterol.
citation: Grundy SM, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.

text: For patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease with an eGFR of 20 mL/min/1.73 m2 or higher, treatment with an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended to slow CKD progression and reduce cardiovascular risk.
citation: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int. 2022;102(5S):S1-S127.

text: An ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker is recommended for patients with diabetes, hypertension, and albuminuria, titrated to the highest approved dose that is tolerated. Monitor serum potassium and creatinine after initiation.
citation: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int. 2022;102(5S):S1-S127.

text: A nonsteroidal mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist such as finerenone is recommended for patients with type 2 diabetes, an eGFR of 25 mL/min/1.73 m2 or higher, normal serum potassium, and albuminuria despite a maximum tolerated dose of a renin-angiotensin system inhibitor.
citation: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int. 2022;102(5S):S1-S127.

text: Metformin may be continued in patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease with an eGFR of 30 mL/min/1.73 m2 or higher; the dose should be reduced at an eGFR below 45 and the drug discontinued when eGFR falls below 30.
citation: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int. 2022;102(5S):S1-S127.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: SGLT2 inhibitors increase the risk of genital mycotic infections and volume depletion, and should be used with caution in patients prone to these conditions. They are associated with a low risk of diabetic ketoacidosis.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.

source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
text: When an ACE inhibitor, angiotensin receptor blocker, or mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist is used, serum potassium should be monitored, as these agents can cause hyperkalemia, particularly in chronic kidney disease.
citation: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Care in Diabetes-2024. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(Suppl 1):S158-S178.
